relevant_files.rationale: Primary orchestration entry point for `fab init`. Coordinates all three initialization stages (bootstrap, mcp, hooks) and writes the four core state files: forensic.json, agents.meta.json, human-lock.json, and bootstrap/README.md.
relevant_files.topic_relation: This file is the architectural root of the init flow. It calls detectClientSupports(), buildForensicReport(), createInitialMeta(), and installBootstrap() in sequence — the complete data lifecycle from discovery to state materialization.

key_code.description: Synchronous fabric state materialization: creates .fabric/ directory structure, runs forensicReport, writes 4 state files atomically. Returns a record of every file action taken.
Bootstraps agents.meta.json with a single L0 node (topology_type=mirror, scope_glob=**). The revision is a sha256 of the bootstrap README hash — establishing the content-addressed revision chain.
Injects agents-md-init-reminder.cjs into .claude/settings.json Stop hooks. Uses atomic write (pid-tmp rename) for settings safety. This is the Claude-specific lifecycle hook wiring.

relevant_files.rationale: Defines the AI client capability matrix — the 7-client detection/configuration system. Determines which clients are present and what init stages apply to each.
relevant_files.topic_relation: Architectural boundary for multi-client extensibility. Adding a new AI client requires only adding an entry here — capability detection is filesystem-based (existsSync checks on known config directories/files), making it open for extension without changing the core init flow.

key_code.description: Returns DetectedClientSupport[] for 7 clients: ClaudeCodeCLI, ClaudeCodeDesktop, Cursor, Windsurf, RooCode, GeminiCLI, CodexCLI. Detection is purely filesystem heuristics (e.g., existsSync(join(homedir(), '.claude'))). Capability map is static per-client.

Factory function returning ClientConfigWriter[] for MCP config installation. Supports explicit path override via FabricConfig.clientPaths, falling back to auto-detected paths. This is the pluggable client adapter layer.
Enum of 4 capability flags: bootstrap, mcp, hook, skill. ClaudeCodeCLI has all 4; all other clients have bootstrap+mcp only. hook and skill are Claude-CLI-exclusive features.

relevant_files.rationale: The forensic engine that builds project cognition at init time. Produces the ForensicReport that becomes forensic.json — the persistent project identity snapshot.
relevant_files.topic_relation: Architectural source of truth for project discovery. The forensic report feeds both the bootstrap guide template selection AND the initial agents.meta.json hash. No AI involvement — fully deterministic static analysis.

key_code.description: Top-level assembly function: runs topology scan, entry point collection, code sampling, assertion building, and candidate file selection in sequence. Validates output against forensicReportSchema before returning.

Produces 6 assertion types: framework, dominant_pattern, entry_directory, meta_sidecar, config, domain. Each assertion has type/statement/confidence/evidence/coverage/proposed_rule. These are the structured cognition artifacts fed into the bootstrap guide.

Selects up to 12 representative files across 5 families (entry, component, config, test, domain) with priority-scored selection. These candidates become the AI's initial read list for understanding the project.

relevant_files.rationale: MCP Server entry point exposing both stdio and HTTP transports. The stdio path is the primary AI client interaction channel; HTTP path serves the dashboard and browser-based MCP clients.
relevant_files.topic_relation: Dual-transport architecture is the central design decision for AI client interaction. stdio is used when the server runs as a child process of the AI client (typical MCP pattern). HTTP is used for the web dashboard and potentially remote AI clients.

key_code.description: Registers 4 MCP tools (fab_get_rules, fab_plan_context, fab_append_intent, fab_update_registry) plus the bootstrap README resource. Returns a McpServer instance not yet connected to any transport.
Connects the MCP server to stdin/stdout. This is the entry point when executed as an MCP server subprocess by Claude/Cursor/etc. Process is run via the config injected during init.
Lazy-imports http.ts and starts Express+MCP on the specified port. Accepts projectRoot, authToken, dashboardDistPath. The dynamic import prevents HTTP dependencies from loading in the stdio path.

relevant_files.rationale: HTTP server combining REST API, SSE event push, and HTTP-based MCP (StreamableHTTP transport) in a single Express app. The architectural hub for all web interactions.
relevant_files.topic_relation: Contains the core reactive architecture: chokidar watches agents.meta.json and bootstrap README, then debounce-notifies all active MCP sessions via tools/list_changed and resource_updated notifications. This is the server-push coupling between file state and AI client perception.

key_code.description: Assembles the full HTTP application: mounts 8 REST API routers, SSE /events endpoint, /mcp endpoint for HTTP MCP, and dashboard static files. Creates a persistent chokidar cache watcher separate from the per-connection SSE watcher.

Watches .fabric/agents.meta.json and .fabric/bootstrap/README.md. On change: invalidates contextCache and debounce-notifies all MCP sessions via sendToolListChanged() or sendResourceUpdated(). This is the hot-path reactivity mechanism.
Broadcasts MCP notifications to all active HTTP sessions. Handles tools/list_changed, resource_updated, resources/list_changed. Best-effort — disconnected sessions are silently skipped.

MCP event replay store backed by .intent-ledger.jsonl. Enables HTTP MCP session reconnection with missed-event replay via replayEventsAfter(). Solves the MCP resumability requirement for HTTP transport.

relevant_files.rationale: The rules resolution engine — core of the AI client perception phase. Implements L0/L1/L2 layered rule delivery with glob-based path matching and staleness detection.
relevant_files.topic_relation: Architectural heart of rule delivery to AI clients. Every time an AI client calls fab_get_rules, this service: reads agents.meta.json (cached), matches nodes by scope_glob against the requested path, partitions results by L0/L1/L2 layer, and appends an audit event. The revision_hash enables client-side stale detection.

key_code.description: Main entry: loads GetRulesContext (cached), computes stale flag by comparing client_hash to meta.revision, resolves rules for path, appends audit event (best-effort, non-blocking). Returns RulesPayload{L0, L1, L2, human_locked_nearby}.
Loads all matching nodes from meta, partitions into L1/L2, returns with L0 content and human lock entries. The dedupeByPath option prevents duplicate rule files in batch scenarios.
Filters meta.nodes by minimatch(path, node.scope_glob), sorts by PRIORITY_ORDER{high:0, medium:1, low:2} then nodeId lexicographically. Reads each matched node's file content from disk.

relevant_files.rationale: SSE event bus for the web dashboard. Watches 4 state files (agents.meta.json, human-lock.json, forensic.json, .intent-ledger.jsonl) and pushes typed FabricEvent notifications to dashboard clients.
relevant_files.topic_relation: Server-to-dashboard reactive channel. The RingBuffer (capacity=50) enables dashboard reconnection without missed events. Lazy watcher lifecycle (start on first connection, stop when all disconnect) is an important scalability constraint for the dashboard.

key_code.description: Returns an HTTP handler that: starts lazy chokidar watcher, replays missed events from RingBuffer using Last-Event-ID, adds client to broadcast set, manages heartbeat (30s ping), and cleans up on disconnect. Enforces CONNECTION_LIMIT=10.
Fixed-capacity circular buffer (capacity=50) storing the last 50 events with sequential numeric IDs. replayFrom(afterId) enables reconnecting clients to catch up without server state loss.

Dispatches file change to specific event reader: AGENTS_META_PATH -> meta:updated, HUMAN_LOCK_PATH -> lock:approved|lock:drift, FORENSIC_PATH -> drift:detected, LEDGER_PATH -> ledger:appended. Each event type carries a different payload schema.

relevant_files.rationale: Unified in-memory cache with three logical slots (meta, context, audit) and two invalidation strategies. Critical for hot-path performance of fab_get_rules under concurrent AI client calls.
relevant_files.topic_relation: The cache is the bridge between file-based state and in-memory performance. Two invalidation paths: meta_write (eager, triggered by updateRegistry) and file_watch (triggered by chokidar). The 5s default TTL provides a safety net against missed invalidations.

key_code.description: Dual-reason invalidation: meta_write clears only metaSlot (node graph changed); file_watch clears both metaSlot and contextSlot (L0 content may have changed too). Scoped to projectRoot or global clear.

relevant_files.rationale: Dashboard REST+SSE client — the complete web interaction surface. Implements all API calls and the SSE stream reader with custom parser.
relevant_files.topic_relation: Architectural boundary between dashboard UI and server. The openSseConnection() function implements a manual SSE parser (not EventSource) to support Bearer token auth headers — EventSource API does not support custom headers. Token handling supports three injection mechanisms: window global, query param, Authorization header.

key_code.description: Manual fetch-based SSE implementation supporting Last-Event-ID for reconnection and Bearer token auth. Uses AbortController for clean teardown. Caller receives onEvent/onOpen/onClose callbacks.

Three-source auth token resolution: window.__FABRIC_AUTH_TOKEN__ global (server-injected), ?token= query param (auto-stripped from URL after read), cached null. Progressive disclosure pattern for different deployment scenarios.

relevant_files.rationale: Defines the core data structure for agents.meta.json and implements the L0/L1/L2 layer derivation logic. This schema is the single source of truth for the node graph structure.
relevant_files.topic_relation: L0/L1/L2 topology is derived from file path heuristics rather than being explicitly authored. Files at .fabric/bootstrap/README.md or AGENTS.md are L0; files with /_cross/ path segment are L1 cross-cutting; depth <= 2 is L1, deeper is L2. topology_type is mirror unless /_cross/ present.

key_code.description: Layer auto-derivation algorithm: L0 for root/bootstrap files, L1 for _cross-cutting segment or depth<=2, L2 for depth>2. Depth measured from .fabric/agents/ prefix stripped path.
Zod schema: {revision: string, nodes: Record<string, AgentsMetaNode>}. The revision is a content hash of all node hashes, enabling efficient stale detection without reading all rule files.

relevant_files.rationale: FabricConfig schema with clientPaths override map, scanIgnores, and auditMode. Controls client detection override and audit strictness.
relevant_files.topic_relation: Extension point for multi-client deployment: clientPaths allows explicit path overrides for any of the 7 clients, bypassing filesystem auto-detection. auditMode (strict/warn/off) controls fab_append_intent compliance enforcement.

relevant_files.rationale: ForensicReport schema — the project identity snapshot persisted as .fabric/forensic.json. Contains topology, entry_points, code_samples, assertions, candidate_files.
relevant_files.topic_relation: The forensic report is both a static snapshot (written at init) and a live drift signal (SSE drift:detected event when file changes). The assertions[] field with proposed_rule strings is the structured output that feeds AI client guidance during bootstrap.

relevant_files.rationale: agents.meta.json reader with caching, typed errors, and FABRIC_PROJECT_ROOT env resolution. The canonical access point for the meta graph across all server services.
relevant_files.topic_relation: resolveProjectRoot() reads FABRIC_PROJECT_ROOT env var, defaulting to process.cwd(). This env var injection (done in createFabricHttpApp) is the mechanism by which the HTTP server serves rules for a specific project when run as a daemon.

relevant_files.rationale: Registry mutation service — the write path for agents.meta.json. Implements add-node, remove-node, update-node operations with atomic file writes and eager cache invalidation.
relevant_files.topic_relation: updateRegistry() is the only sanctioned write path to agents.meta.json from the AI client (via fab_update_registry). Uses atomicWriteText (tmp file + rename) for consistency. computeRevision() recalculates the content-addressed revision hash after each write.

key_code.description: Read-modify-write cycle for agents.meta.json: reads current meta (cached), applies operation, computes new revision as sha256 of all sorted node hashes, atomically writes, eagerly invalidates meta cache. Returns new revision_hash.

relevant_files.rationale: Generates .fabric/bootstrap/README.md content by combining a framework-specific markdown template with project name and framework kind. The L0 bootstrap content is the root of the AI client instruction hierarchy.
relevant_files.topic_relation: Template selection is framework-driven (cocos/vite/next have specific templates, others get generic AGENTS.md.template). The scan report from createScanReport() (which calls the forensic engine) feeds directly into template selection. This is the data transformation boundary between forensic cognition and human-readable instructions.

key_code.description: Calls createScanReport() to get framework kind, selects template from AGENTS_TEMPLATE_BY_FRAMEWORK map, replaces {projectName} and {frameworkKind} tokens. Returns final bootstrap markdown string.

key_findings.title: Data Flow: Four-Stage Pipeline with Content-Addressed State
key_findings.description: The init data flow is a linear four-stage pipeline: (1) Forensic scan builds ForensicReport [forensic.json] — deterministic static analysis of filesystem topology, (2) Bootstrap guide generation selects framework-specific template and produces L0 markdown [.fabric/bootstrap/README.md], (3) Initial meta creation computes sha256(bootstrapContent) and seeds agents.meta.json with a single L0 node, (4) Bootstrap/MCP/hooks installation propagates references to AI client config files. The revision hash in agents.meta.json is a content-addressed compound hash of all node hashes, enabling efficient stale detection without reading rule file contents.

key_findings.title: Communication Architecture: Three Distinct Channels with Different Guarantees
key_findings.description: Three communication channels exist between components: (1) CLI-to-MCP (stdio): Used when AI client spawns the fabric server as a subprocess. Stateless per-message, no reconnection concern. (2) Dashboard-to-Server (HTTP REST + SSE): REST for point-in-time queries; SSE for push notifications with RingBuffer replay (capacity=50, Last-Event-ID based). (3) AI-Client-to-Server (HTTP MCP via StreamableHTTP): Session-based with JsonlEventStore for event replay on reconnect. The critical asymmetry: stdio MCP has no reconnection/replay mechanism; HTTP MCP has full JSONL-backed event store.

key_findings.title: State Management: File-Based Single Source of Truth with In-Memory Cache Layer
key_findings.description: agents.meta.json is the authoritative state. All reads go through ContextCache (5s TTL, keyed by projectRoot). Two invalidation paths: eager invalidation via contextCache.invalidate('meta_write') after updateRegistry(), and reactive invalidation via contextCache.invalidate('file_watch') when chokidar detects on-disk changes. The cache is a module-level singleton shared across all HTTP requests. Consistency guarantee: within a 5s window, concurrent reads may see stale state — there is no read-your-writes guarantee between updateRegistry() and the next getRules() call in a different request.

key_findings.title: Component Coupling: Shared Package as Tight Coupling Boundary
key_findings.description: The @fenglimg/fabric-shared package is the only explicit dependency boundary between cli, server, and dashboard. Both cli and server import agentsMetaSchema, forensicReportSchema, and type definitions. The dashboard imports only types (no runtime schemas). This creates a 'shared kernel' coupling where any schema change in fabric-shared is a breaking change for all consumers. There is no versioned API contract between packages — they evolve together as a monorepo. Replacing the dashboard would require reimplementing the SSE client protocol (documented in api/client.ts) but no other server-side changes. Adding a new AI client requires only adding an entry to resolver.ts and a corresponding ClientConfigWriter implementation.

key_findings.title: Cache Invalidation Architecture: Dual Watcher Separation
key_findings.description: There are TWO separate chokidar watchers with different scopes and lifecycles: (1) cacheWatcher in http.ts — persistent, watches agents.meta.json + bootstrap/README.md, invalidates ContextCache and sends MCP tool/resource notifications to active sessions. (2) Per-connection watcher in api/events.ts — lazy lifecycle (starts on first SSE client connect, stops when all disconnect), watches all 4 state files, pushes typed FabricEvent to dashboard clients. The duplication means changes to agents.meta.json trigger both MCP session notifications AND dashboard SSE events, with separate debounce timers (200ms for MCP vs 75ms for SSE).

key_findings.title: Scalability Constraints: Single-Process, Single-Project Architecture
key_findings.description: The current architecture is fundamentally single-process and single-project: FABRIC_PROJECT_ROOT is set via process.env (one value per process), contextCache is a module-level singleton (no namespacing beyond projectRoot key which is always the same in practice), and the SSE connection limit is hardcoded at 10. For monorepos with 100+ modules, the glob-based rule matching (minimatch over all meta.nodes) is O(N*M) where N=nodes and M=files-per-session. For 20+ AI clients, the per-session McpServer instantiation in createSession() means each connection creates a full server instance in memory with no shared state beyond the event store.

key_findings.title: AI Client Detection: Filesystem Heuristics with Explicit Override Escape Hatch
key_findings.description: Client detection is purely filesystem-based: check for ~/.claude, ~/.gemini, ~/.codex, or workspace-local .cursor/, .windsurf/, .roo/ directories. No version detection, no capability probing. The fabricConfigSchema.clientPaths field provides an explicit override map for all 7 clients, enabling explicit configuration in CI/CD or unusual setups. Only ClaudeCodeCLI has the full capability set (bootstrap+mcp+hook+skill); all others lack hook and skill support — a significant capability asymmetry.

key_findings.title: Rule Delivery Protocol: Revision-Based Stale Detection with Audit Trail
key_findings.description: Every fab_get_rules call returns revision_hash and stale boolean (stale = client_hash != current revision). This enables AI clients to detect that the rule graph has changed since their last call and re-fetch. Each call is also appended to an audit log (best-effort, non-blocking catch). The audit log and the intent ledger (.intent-ledger.jsonl) together form a full compliance audit trail: when rules were fetched, what files were targeted, and what AI actions followed.

code_anchors.significance: The data transformation chain: forensicReport -> bootstrapContent (framework-templated) -> bootstrapHash -> meta.revision. Shows how forensic scan output becomes the seed for the content-addressed revision system.

L0/L1/L2 layer classification is path-depth-based, not explicit. This means the rule graph topology is emergent from file placement conventions — a key architectural constraint that limits rule organization flexibility.

The reactive MCP notification chain: file change -> cache invalidation + debounced session notification. awaitWriteFinish prevents partial-read race conditions. NOTIFY_DEBOUNCE_MS=200 is the MCP notification latency budget.
Rule matching is O(N) minimatch glob per node, no indexing. For large node graphs (100+ nodes in monorepos), every fab_get_rules call scans all nodes. Primary scalability bottleneck.

Fixed-capacity 50-event ring buffer for SSE reconnection. If a client reconnects after >50 events were emitted, it loses events. No persistent storage for SSE events (unlike MCP events which have JSONL store).

Three-slot cache design separates concerns: meta (raw AgentsMeta), context (resolved GetRulesContext including L0 file content), audit (streaming offset cursor). The meta_write path invalidates only metaSlot to preserve the L0 content cache when only node graph changed.

chain: contextCache.get('context', projectRoot) -> cache hit or miss
contextCache.get('meta', projectRoot) -> cache hit or miss
return {revision_hash, stale, rules: {L0, L1, L2, human_locked_nearby}}
server.sendToolListChanged() for each active McpServer session [http.ts:L244]
ensureWatcher() -> start chokidar if no clients
add res to clients set, start heartbeat timer
manual SSE parser: buf accumulation, \n\n boundary splitting, parseSseBlock()

questions_for_user.question: agents.meta.json currently uses a single L0 node after init. What is the expected workflow for adding L1/L2 nodes — is this done manually by the human, automatically by the AI client via fab_update_registry, or through a guided interview process?
questions_for_user.context: The init command creates only a single L0 node. The fab_update_registry tool allows AI clients to add nodes, but there's no guided interview or scaffold beyond the initial bootstrap guide.

questions_for_user.question: The bootstrap stage in installBootstrap() appears to mark all targets as 'skipped' (no-op stub per research.json). Is this intentional (handled separately) or an incomplete implementation?
questions_for_user.context: research.json notes: 'Bootstrap install is a no-op stub'. The init flow calls installBootstrap() but the core bootstrap file (README.md) is written by initFabric() earlier. It's unclear what installBootstrap() is supposed to do additionally.

questions_for_user.question: The SSE RingBuffer has a hard cap of 50 events and no persistent backing. Is this acceptable for the dashboard use case, or should missed events (e.g., during an extended disconnect) trigger a full state refresh instead of attempting replay?
questions_for_user.context: If >50 events fire while the dashboard is disconnected, clients will miss events silently. The HTTP MCP channel uses JSONL-backed replay but the dashboard SSE does not.

questions_for_user.question: For the planned 20+ AI client scale: is the current model of one McpServer instance per HTTP session acceptable, or should server instances be pooled/shared across sessions for the same projectRoot?
questions_for_user.context: Each new HTTP MCP session creates a new createFabricServer() instance (4 tool registrations, 1 resource). For 20+ concurrent AI clients this is 20+ McpServer instances in memory sharing no state except via contextCache.

questions_for_user.question: The L0/L1/L2 layer is derived purely from file path depth. Is this emergent convention sufficient, or is there a need for explicit layer declaration in agents.meta.json for cases where depth doesn't match desired semantics?
questions_for_user.context: deriveAgentsMetaLayer() uses directory depth and _cross segment presence. A file at .fabric/agents/domain/rules.md would be L2 by depth, but might be intended as L1 cross-cutting content.

key_patterns_identified: Content-addressed revision chain (sha256 of node hashes)
File-based state with in-memory cache overlay
Forensic-driven bootstrap (no AI in init flow)
